coredata.dc:title: Controllable synthesis of bifunctional magnetic carbon dots for rapid fluorescent detection and reversible removal of Hg2+
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Ying
Xie, Ruyan
Tian, Miaomiao
Mao, Baodong
Chai, Fang
coredata.dc:description: Mercury is one of the most toxic heavy metals, whose identification and separation are crucial for environmental remediation. Till now, it remains a significant challenge upon simultaneous detection and removal of Hg2+. Herein, bifunctional probe magnetic carbon dots were synthesized and optimized via systematic structure manipulation of the carbon and iron precursors towards fluorescence, Hg2+ adsorption and magnetic separation. The probe exhibited blue emission at 440 nm with high quantum yield of 55 % and a high paramagnetism with the saturation magnetization value of 22.70 emu/g. Furthermore, the fluorescent detection of Hg2+ with limit of 5.40 nM and high selectivity were achieved through surface structure manipulation with moderate -NH2, -SH and Fe contents. As a result, the magnetic removal of Hg2+ was consecutively effectuated with high removal efficiency of 98.30 %. The detection and recovery of Hg2+ in real samples were further verified and demonstrated the excellent environmental tolerance of probe. The reusability was viable with recycling at least three turns by external magnet. This work not only provides a promising approach for simultaneous detection and removal of heavy metal pollution, but also provides an excellent example as a versatile platform for multifunction integration via the structure manipulation for other applications.

(c) Magnetization hysteresis loops of Fe3O4 NPs and M-CDs measured at room temperature. The curve and image of a, b and c in the picture represented GSH-CDs, Fe3O4 NPs and M-CDs, respectively.

FL spectra of M-CDs fabricated under different (a) molar ratios of GSH/AFC, (b) reaction times, and (c) reaction temperatures. Insets in (a-c): the corresponding FL intensity data. The quenching efficiency of M-CDs with various GSH/AFC precursor ratios by (d) Hg2+, (e) Cu2+, and (f) Ag+. FT-IR spectra of the M-CDs and with (g) Hg2+, (h) Cu2+ and (i) Ag+.

(a) TEM image of the as-prepared M-CDs, inset: size distribution. (b) HRTEM image of M-CDs (the blue and red dotted circles signing Fe3O4 and CDs, respectively.). (c) The XRD pattern of M-CDs in comparison with Fe3O4 (JCPDS No. 77–1545). (d) FT-IR spectra of M-CDs and the GSH precursor. High resolution XPS spectra of (e) C 1s, (f) N 1s, (g) O 1s, (h) S 2p and (i) Fe 2p.

(a) UV–vis absorption (black) and ﬂuorescence excitation (red) and emission (blue) spectra of M-CDs. Inset: photographs of the M-CDs under daylight (left) and UV light irradiation at 365 nm (right). (b) Two-dimensional excitation-emission map of M-CDs. (c) FL spectra of M-CDs under different excitation wavelengths. (d) FL decay curves of M-CDs at 440 nm.

FL emission spectra of M-CDs against different (a) pH, (b) concentration of NaCl, (c) UV irradiation time, and (d) storing time. Insets: the corresponding I/I0 ratios and photographs of the M-CDs under UV light.

(a) The fluorescent intensity ratio I/I0 of M-CDs with various metal ions (100 μM) vs. Hg2+ (100 μM). (b) The corresponding sample image under UV light. (c) FL spectra of M-CDs exposing to different concentrations of Hg2+ (0–100 μM). (d) The linear fitting of I0/I vs. the Hg2+ concentration (0.008–0.08 μM, R2 = 0.999). Inset in d: corresponding sample image under UV light.

(a) The removal behavior of by the wet process: concentration of Hg2+ in different water samples tested by ICP-OES (I: ultrapure water+Hg2+, II: ultrapure water+Hg2++M-CDs solution, III: ultrapure water+Fe3O4 +Hg2+). (b) Removal of Hg2+ by wet process at different temperatures. (c) The removal behavior of by the dry process and reusable cycles.

(c) FT-IR spectra of M-CDs without (black line) and with Hg2+ (red). (d) UV–vis absorption spectra of M-CDs without (black) and with (red) Hg2+. XPS spectra of (e) N 1s, (f) O 1s, and (g) S 2p of M-CDs+Hg2+. (h) XPS spectra of Hg 4f. (i) Schematic mechanism for the binding of M-CDs to Hg2+.

head.ce:title: Controllable synthesis of bifunctional magnetic carbon dots for rapid fluorescent detection and reversible removal of Hg2+

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mercury is one of the most toxic heavy metals, whose identification and separation are crucial for environmental remediation. Till now, it remains a significant challenge upon simultaneous detection and removal of Hg2+. Herein, bifunctional probe magnetic carbon dots were synthesized and optimized via systematic structure manipulation of the carbon and iron precursors towards fluorescence, Hg2+ adsorption and magnetic separation. The probe exhibited blue emission at 440 nm with high quantum yield of 55 % and a high paramagnetism with the saturation magnetization value of 22.70 emu/g. Furthermore, the fluorescent detection of Hg2+ with limit of 5.40 nM and high selectivity were achieved through surface structure manipulation with moderate -NH2, -SH and Fe contents. As a result, the magnetic removal of Hg2+ was consecutively effectuated with high removal efficiency of 98.30 %. The detection and recovery of Hg2+ in real samples were further verified and demonstrated the excellent environmental tolerance of probe. The reusability was viable with recycling at least three turns by external magnet. This work not only provides a promising approach for simultaneous detection and removal of heavy metal pollution, but also provides an excellent example as a versatile platform for multifunction integration via the structure manipulation for other applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis of (a) GSH-CDs, (b) Fe3O4 NPs, and (c) M-CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals pose a great threat to world environment and human health due to their hypertoxicity, genotoxicity, carcinogenicity and non-biodegradable nature [3,43]. Among them, Hg2+ is recognized as one of the most hazardous pollutions and carcinogens because of its solubility and environmental bioaccumulation [27,8,9]. Even trace-level Hg2+ also can cause kidney, immune system and other disease in animals and human, which can cause great threat to the environment ecology and human health [2,28,7]. The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) strictly stipulates that the maximum allowable level of Hg2+ in drinking water is 10 nM [52]. Considering all forms of mercury species (metallic, inorganic and organic) are harmful to human organisms and environment, removal of Hg2+ from water bodies is urgently needed [39,40]. For the removal of Hg2+, the existence in water should be determined as a major premise. Therefore, it has become a focus of research to figure out the problems related to the simultaneous determination and separation of Hg2+ from waters, achieved water remediation.

For detection of Hg2+, conventional approaches such as atomic absorption spectroscopy and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [32] could not achieve on-site detection in resource-limited environment for their reliance on instruments. In recent years, many endeavors especially fluorescent nano-sensors have been made to simplify the detection process and improve the effectiveness, including nanoclusters, and quantum dots, and CDs [24]. Among them, the CDs were endowed considerable concern due to their fascinating optical properties, unti-photobleaching, facile synthesis, low toxicity, easy modification, and widely available cheap precursors [19,4,61]. These merits have rendered them great attractive in sensors, bioimaging, and adsorbents. More recently, modified CDs with emblazoned properties have been fabricated and functionalized by doping elements such as Cd, N and S [58] or combining with magnetic NPs (Fe3O4-CDs) [55]. These functional CDs bolstered the sensing heavy metals and derived more coveted ability, which has a beneficial effect on the application in extensive field [27,58].

For the separation process, various techniques including chemical precipitation, ion exchange, adsorption, electrochemical technology, and membrane filtration have been developed for the removal of Hg2+ [16,50,60], among them, the adsorption by using magnetic nanomaterial adsorbents is particularly attractive, due to its low cost, high efficiency, simple operation, few generations of by-products and reusability [11,13]. Recently, Fe3O4 based magnetic nanoparticles was rated as excellent adsorbents for heavy metal ions separation due to ease of synthesis and with availability of resources [26,51]. Some adsorbents like core-shell Fe3O4@SiO2, cellolose@Fe3O4, rGO@Fe3O4, and polymer-capped Fe3O4 NPs have been explored for removal of Hg2+ [21,33,59]. Noteworthily, they exhibited potent separation performance with reliable reusability, which offers them high expectant for heavy metals treatment.

If the detection and removal of Hg2+ can be completed simultaneously, the great potential in protecting the environment will be double gained. Accordingly, it is expected to design a magnetic fluorescent probe to achieve the detection and consecutive removal of Hg2+ with high selectivity, accuracy and magnetic separating function simultaneously. Recently, magnetic CDs with adjustable functional groups have been involved in detection and separation of radioactive ions innovatively [53] . However, it is great challenging to ensure the good effect of both magnetism and fluorescence (FL) simultaneously, because of the quenching effect of the magnetic aggregation, which could cause the failing for testing [25]. To avoid magnetic aggregation, the designing of magnetic CDs composites has been widely explored, such as core shell SiO2 [21], MOFs [10], COFs [20] and molecular imprinted polymer hybrids [25] Nevertheless, except the complex construction process, high cost, and loss of magnetization, the low selectivity is the fatal weakness, which hinders the development of complex structure of magnetic CDs composites. Feasibly, the hierarchical magnetic Fe3O4-CDs have been concerned as promising materials for separation of Hg2+ with high efficiency, which deriving from their high selectivity [15], and integrated optical and magnetic properties. Specifically, the combination of magnetic-CDs can be considered as creating the synergism of key functions for the monitoring and removal [15]. Thus, it is of great significance to integrate the FL of CDs and magnetic property optimally and reasonably, to develop a facile, energetic, effective FL probe for detection and removal of Hg2+ simultaneously. In this respect, we have attempted to synthesize Fe3O4-CDs by hydrothermal method and materialized both detection and removal of Hg2+ [46,56], both the efficiency of removal and limit of detection need to be further optimized and improved. Noticeably, the FL of CDs are closely influenced by carbon core, surface, molecular state and dopants [29]. Their optical properties can be modulated by incorporating heteroatoms (such as N, S) and surface decorating. To fulfill high efficient bifunctional detection and removal of Hg2+, the fabricating can be actualized by adjusting the resource of magnetism and carbon through intramolecular mixing of multi-component doping and appropriate surface decorating.

In this study, the glutathione, diethylenetriamine and ammonium ferric citrate were respectively favored as resource of S, N and Fe, to integrate magnetic CDs with virtues of high QY, paramagnetism and high selectivity for Hg2+. The simultaneous introducing multiple elements and intramolecular mixing at hydrothermal condition ensured ultra-small size of magnetic nanoparticles, which was expected no impact to optical property of CDs. Further, the surface functional groups of CDs including -NH2, -SH and -COOH can be optimized by tuning the ratio of doping elements, which maximized the advantages of both magnetic nature and FL of products. The dual-functional probe exhibited a sustainable recycle performance and can be reused to separate Hg2+ for at least 3 times. The FL intensity of M-CDs quenched evidently with the addition of Hg2+ on account of static quenching effect (SQE) and internal filtration effect (IFE). Accordingly, a fluorometric strategy for Hg2+ detection was built up with high sensitivity, selectivity and prompt response time. It is emphasizing that the magnetic removal process can also accomplish the separation of Hg2+ from the polluted water, demonstrating great potential for application in environmental pollution monitoring and removal.

The M-CDs was synthesized and optimized by tuning the two main precursors GSH and AFC with different molar ratio. The ratio of precursors GSH/AFC was individually estimated with 1:5, 2:5, 3:5, 4:5 and 5:5, the evaluation of reaction temperature (160–240 °C) and reaction time (4–14 h) were further performed. Their magnetic and fluorescent properties were subsequently studied and evaluated to determine the optimal synthesized conditions. In a typical optimal process, AFC (0.2441 g, 0.5 mmol) and GSH (0.1228 g, 0.4 mmol) were dissolved in a mixed solvent with H2O and DETA (volume ratio 9:1, 10 mL) as a precursor solution. Then the homogeneous solution was transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. After reacting at 180 °C for 8 h, the products were cooled down to room temperature and then centrifuged (6000 rpm, 10 min) to collect the solid phase and supernatant. The solids were ultrasonically dispersed into an ethanol phase (4 mL, repeated 3 times with 3 min interval) and finally dried at 60 °C for 5 h. The obtained supernatant and powder were stored in the refrigerator (4 °C) for further use.

All FL spectra of detection were performed at room temperature. To investigate the FL response of M-CDs on Hg2+, the M-CDs supernatant was diluted 2000 times as the fluorescent probe. Then different Hg2+ samples (150 μL, 0–100 μM) were mixed with 150 μL of the probe solutions. After incubating for 3 min, the FL intensity of the mixtures was measured with excitation wavelength at 360 nm. For the evaluation of specificity, 100 μM of other analogical metal ions (Ag+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Cr3+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Co2+ and Cd2+) were exposed to the probe instead of Hg2+ for FL assay. To validate the efficiency and accuracy of the probe, real water samples (Yellow Sea water from Yantai City in Shandong Province, and tap water from our campus) were collected and investigated. Prior to use, all samples were filtered through a syringe (0.22 µm) to remove impurities. Then the Hg2+ concentrations in real water samples were analyzed by M-CDs firstly, and subsequently by standard addition method.

The magnetic removal of Hg2+ was performed by two different strategies: wet and dry processes. For the wet process, the M-CDs was diluted 100 times and employed as probe, which was mixed with Hg2+ solutions (20 ppm) with 1:1 vol ratio. After complete dispersion, the M-CDs with Hg2+ was separated by a permanent magnet, and the supernatant was simultaneously obtained to certify the removal effect of Hg2+ by ICP-OES. The dry process is simpler, where 0.02 g of M-CDs powder was mixed with the Hg2+ solution (40 mL, 10 ppm). Then the mixture was stirred for 24 h, before magnetic separation and supernatant collection for ICP-OES test. To verify the reusability, the magnetically separated M-CDs with Hg2+ from the dry process was restored by washing with saturated EDTA solution to detach the adsorbed Hg2+. Then the recovered M-CDs was recycled for Hg2+ removal test in the next turn, which was repeated for three times.

To fabricate dual-functional M-CDs with fluorescent and magnetic properties, the M-CDs should be consisted of two components: CDs for offering fluorescent sensing function and Fe3O4 nanoparticles for providing the effect of magnetic separation. Our proposed protocol is emphasized two main strategies. One is tuning and optimizing the co-existence magnetism and FL properties by doping N, S, Fe as dopants. And another is modulation of surface functionalization by tuning the ratio of precursor to optimize the surface modifying groups for the promotion of selectivity. The synthesis and optimization of M-CDs can be illustrated in the Fig. 1. The M-CDs was synthesized by one-step solvothermal method using AFC as iron source, GSH as nitrogen and sulfur source, DETA as nitrogen source, and all reactant can be treated as carbon source (Fig. 1a). Moreover, the GSH-CDs and Fe3O4 NPs were prepared by precursor of GSH and AFC solely at same conditions to compare and evaluate their fluorescent and magnetic properties. The GSH-CDs represented weaker FL than M-CDs, and no FL could not be found in Fe3O4 NPs (Fig. 1b), indicated the excellent FL property originated from the synergy from multi-element doping. The saturation magnetization value of the M-CDs and Fe3O4 NPs were 22.70 emu/g and 13.33 emu/g respectively (Fig. 1c), confirming their paramagnetism and denoting the M-CDs exhibited more better magnetism than pure Fe3O4 NPs. The magnetic property of M-CDs was further proved by external magnet, the photograph of the M-CDs (Fig. 1c, inset) depicted that all the brownish black particles can be attracted and aggregated within 30 s, suggesting the M-CDs can be separated by magnetic field effectively. This result ensured their possibility for magnetic separation.

To ensure the M-CDs possessing both excellent emission and strong magnetism, the appropriate optimization conditions were evaluated. The synthesis with different molar ratios of GSH/AFC (1:5, 2:5, 3:5, 4:5 and 5:5) ( Fig. 2a), various temperatures (160–240 °C) (Fig. 2b) and reaction time (4–14 h) (Fig. 2c) were estimated, respectively. Appropriate reaction temperature and time promoted the generation of more carbon cores, which can be got to form the M-CDs with enlarging carbon cores, caused the weakness of FL intensity of the M-CDs [45]. And rational GSH/AFC molar ratio is more beneficial to both formation of fluorophore and their abundant surface functional groups, which determined their properties such as coordination ability, optical stability and favored them to apply as outstanding probe for Hg2+ [54]. Thus, the optimum synthesized condition can be assured at GSH/AFC molar ratio 4:5 and reaction for 8 h at 180 °C. The molecular structure of GSH and AFC illustrated abundant carboxyl groups, thiol group, and amino group could be distributed on the surface of M-CDs. By virtue of abundant functional groups and magnetic property, the M-CDs were qualified the detection and separation for Hg2+ in environmental water.

The sensing performance matching the synthesis of M-CDs was further evaluated by estimating the interference of detection between M-CDs and relevant metals. The crucial selectivity should be considered carefully due to the specificity played a significant role and concerned to the accuracy of detection and recovery in practical application. As delineated in Fig. S1, the interference of other ions can be ignored due to no influence on fluorescent intensity except Cu2+. However, as the structural similarity elements with Hg2+, Cu2+ and Ag+ could cause effect on real environmental detection. The optimizing selectivity could be achieved by tuning abundant functional groups such as -COOH, -NH2, -OH and –SH on M-CDs surface. The M-CDs with variable ratio of GSH: AFC (1:5, 2:5, 3:5, 4:5 and 5:5) was further investigated to evaluate the effect of sensing performance. All the fluorescent intensity can be extinguished with the Hg2+ (Fig. 2d-f), while different M-CDs samples exhibited various performance with Cu2+ and Ag+, manifested the distinct interference caused by Cu2+ and Ag+ and seated their different selectivity. The various ratio of GSH and AFC provided proportionate chance of assigning the functional groups distributing on the surface of M-CDs, which achieved various sensing behavior. In comparison, the M-CDs obtained with n(GSH): n(AFC) (4:5) exhibited better quenching effect for Hg2+ (Fig. 2d) and minimum interference from Cu2+ (Fig. 2e) and Ag+ (Fig. 2f), indicated the optimized ratio of precursors can be determined at n(GSH): n(AFC) = 4:5.

To further illustrate the selectivity behavior of M-CDs, the FT-IR of three M-CDs samples combined with their preferentially coordinated Hg2+, Cu2+ and Ag+ were compared and discussed. The bands at 1043 cm−1, 1124 cm−1 and 1404 cm−1 associated to the stretching vibration of C-O, C-S and the bending mode of C-N, these three peaks occurred obviously blue shift with Hg2+, indicated the Hg2+ bonded with M-CDs via -SH, C-N and C-O (Fig. 2g). Notably, beside the evident blue shift, the -CO stretching vibration at 1655 cm−1 weakened evidently, confirming the Hg2+ occurred strong interaction with CO. The coordination between above functional groups on M-CDs with Hg2+ caused the extinction of FL. In comparison, the peak at 1045 cm−1 of M-CDs (C-O vibration) with Cu2+ kept no shift with slight decrease. While the stretching vibration of C-N at 1300 cm−1 and C-S stretching vibration at 1084 cm−1 changed evidently, especially C-S stretching vibration strengthened and shifted to 1126 cm−1, indicated the quenching of M-CDs caused by Cu2+ was achieved though the coordination by -SH and C-N groups. The Ag+ can occur coordination by C-N and C-S groups due to the strong transformation of 1404 cm−1 and 1298 cm−1 (C-N vibration), the C-S stretching vibration at 1115 cm−1 shifted to 1089 cm−1. The variation of FT-IR of M-CDs with Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ were devoted to illustrate their selectivity caused by surface functional groups tuning by changing the ratio of precursors. The optimum of surface groups achieved by M-CDs synthesized with n(GSH): n(AFC) (4:5) can promote the selectivity for Hg2+ and avoid the interference of Cu2+ and Ag+ simultaneously.

The morphology and size of the M-CDs were evaluated by TEM ( Fig. 3a), which manifested M-CDs were approximately spherical shape with uniform size. The particles size distribution gave a scale of size within 1–5 nm, with mean size of 2.57 ± 0.07 nm (Fig. 3a, inset). HRTEM image of the M-CDs (Fig. 3b) displayed their high crystallinity, whose interplanar distance can be found at 0.25 nm and 0.30 nm, corresponding to the (3 1 1) crystal plane spacing of Fe3O4 and the (0 0 2) lattice plane of graphene, respectively [56]. The clear profile of Fe3O4 and CDs nanoparticles can be noticed, manifested the main component of M-CDs is a hybrid structure composed by Fe3O4 NPs and CDs. The subminiature structure of Fe3O4 NPs maintained the magnetic nature and avoided the influence on FL, which made M-CDs holding the separation utility in the recovery of Hg2+ besides detection.

The XRD analysis of the M-CDs exposes an extensive peak at 2θ = 23° (Fig. 3c), confirming the sp2 hybrid C-core structure of the CDs [53]. The broad diffraction pattern also disclosed that the as-prepared M-CDs are small in size, which is in accord with the TEM result [31]. The weak diffraction peaks at 18.02°, 30.08°, 35.53°, 43.30°, 57.23° and 62.85° in the XRD patterns are consistent with the crystal planes (1 1 1), (2 2 0), (3 1 1), (4 0 0), (5 1 1) and (4 4 0) of Fe3O4 (JCPDS card No. 77–1545). The peak at 35.53° can be ascribed to the (3 1 1) facet of the Fe3O4, which is perfectly matched the HRTEM result [46]. The XRD analysis further verified the hybrid structure of Fe3O4 and CDs components of M-CDs.

To analyze the surface functional groups, elemental compositions of M-CDs, and their coordination with Hg2+, FT-IR and XPS were initially recorded (Supporting information). Compared with precursor GSH, the stretching vibration of CO of the carboxyl group disappears at 1711 cm−1, the band at 1655 cm−1 is indicative of CO (amide I) stretching vibration [14,58]. And a characteristic peak at 586 cm−1 in the M-CDs represented Fe-O functional group of Fe3O4, confirmed the components of magnets [5]. Furthermore, the strong absorption band (-SH) of GSH at 2525 cm−1 disappeared in the M-CDs indicated the function between the Fe3O4 and CDs [49]. Above results successfully demonstrated the well doping of S and N in the M-CDs, and the optimized active functional groups (-COOH, -NH2, C-S and -SH) determined their capability of chelating Hg2+ and further separation from the environment [15,29].

The surface composition and valence state of the M-CDs was further testified by XPS analysis. The full spectrum (Fig. S2) showed five pinnacles lying C 1 s (285.2 eV), N 1 s (399.3 eV), O 1 s (529.9 eV), S 2p (163.2 eV) and Fe 2p (710.5 eV), with amount of C (24.86 %, Fig. 3e), N (9.30 %, Fig. 3f), O (41.32 %, Fig. 3g), S (3.62 %), and Fe (20.91 %) (Supporting information), indicated the comparative amount of C and Fe components and presence of N and S. The S 2p peaks (Fig. 3h) situated at 163.3, 164.5, 168.0, and 169.1 eV, related to S 2p3/2, S 2p1/2, C-SO2-C and C-O-SO2-C, further proved the S doping in M-CDs [6,35]. The Fe 2p spectra (Fig. 3i) showed two peaks at 723.5 eV and 710.2 eV can be assigned to Fe (II) 2p1/2 and Fe (II) 2p3/2, and the peaks at 726.1 eV and 712.8 eV attributed to Fe (III) 2p1/2 and Fe (III) 2p3/2, the peaks located at 718.7 eV and 732.1 eV were satellite peaks of Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 [1]. These results verified the components of Fe3O4, echoed the XRD results exactly. The XPS analysis further confirmed the precise contents and affluent surface decoration of M-CDs, in good agreement with the FT-IR data.

Subsequently, the optical properties of M-CDs were successively evaluated. The M-CDs have a weak absorption peak at 242 nm ( Fig. 4a), in accord with the π–π * transition of CC bonds, satisfying with the condition of producing blue FL [46]. In the lower-energy region, a wide shoulder near 353 nm was ascribed to the n-π * transitions of CO or CN groups [58], which can improve spin-orbit coupling, facilitating intersystem crossing and triplet excitation generation [36]. The M-CDs solution exhibited pale yellow color in daylight (left), and emitted bright blue FL under 365 nm UV illumination (right) (Fig. 4a, inset). The ﬂuorescence spectra of the M-CDs exhibited a maximum emission at 440 nm with the optimal excitation wavelength of 360 nm (Fig. 4a, b), which was evaluated by the study of excitation dependent ﬂuorescence behavior. The emission maintained at 440 nm with different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 4c), indicated their uniform surface states of M-CDs [12]. The QY of the M-CDs was determined to be 55 % by a typical approach by choosing quinine sulfate as the reference [62]. The superior QY of M-CDs can be ascribed to the surface passivation and functionalization of CDs [63]. Further, the average ﬂuorescence lifetime of M-CDs monitored at 440 nm was 9.38 ns (Fig. 4d). These results demonstrated excellent optical properties of M-CDs, which maintained their outstanding behavior in sensing.

Considering that the fluorescent stability of probes is a key concern for the sensing behavior and practical applications of fluorescent sensors, the FL emission of M-CDs under different conditions were further estimated. The FL intensity of the M-CDs boosted continuously along with rising of pH within 3.00–6.00 ( Fig. 5a), indicated the optical property was influenced by some pH sensitive surface modifying groups [57]. When the pH value ranged from 6.00 to 10.83, M-CDs kept no variation, indicating a relatively stable state of the FL. To ensure the sensing ability in different pH, the quenching efficiency of M-CDs for Hg2+ at different pH was also tested (Fig. S3). The results indicated the performance of M-CDs did not occur evident variation, indicated their good pH tolerance. As portrayed in Fig. 5b, no obvious FL influence of M-CDs with high concentration of NaCl, illustrated their excellent salt resistance. Afterwards, the ultraviolet exposure time and storage time were also evaluated. The results manifested the FL intensity of M-CDs kept unchanged under continuous UV radiation for 3 h, suggesting their high capacity for resistance to photobleaching (Fig. 5c). Similarly, the M-CDs exhibited tiny attenuation on FL intensity after storing for 30 days at 4 °C (Fig. 5d), signified their favorable optical stability. These phenomena may be on account of the surface functional groups and stable chemical structure of M-CDs [48]. Above results demonstrated the M-CDs possessing outstanding optical stability, which benefit for their application in practical application.

The sensing performance of M-CDs for Hg2+ was investigated by evaluating their selectivity and sensitivity. Considering that the influence of potential other metal ions existed in real samples on the FL examining method for Hg2+, the specificity and anti-interference capacity of M-CDs were estimated. Based on above optimizing results, the selectivity can be ensured in this work. The FL responses of M-CDs to potential interferences including 100 μM of Ag+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Cr3+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Cd2+ and Hg2+ were investigated. In contrast, the relative FL intensity (I/I0) plunged only in the presence of Hg2+ ( Fig. 6a), and when introduced Hg2+ the FL intensity of other samples slumped dramatically. Simultaneously, corresponding emission of M-CDs were exhausted under UV light (Fig. 6b). These results manifested outstanding selectivity and anti-interference capability of M-CDs.

The incubation time was determined at 3 min after investigating within 10 min, due to the response time can be regarded as pivotal factor for probe (Fig. S4), implied M-CDs possessed a fast response to Hg2+ to achieve rapid on-site assay. The performance of M-CDs for Hg2+ detection was consecutively evaluated in detail. As plotted in Fig. 6c, upon gradually increasing Hg2+ concentrations from 0.008 to 100 μM, a slight red shift and elimination of emission can be noticed, which matched with the variation of FL intensity under UV light (Fig. 6d, inset), indicated that the emission exhibited the dependency on the concentration of Hg2+. The corresponding linear equation was established as I0/I = 1.59x+ 1.01 (Fig. 6d), with the linear fitting in the range of 0.008 ∼0.08 μM (R2 = 0.999) (I0 and I represented FL intensity of blank and M-CDs+ Hg2+ respectively). The LOD of Hg2+ was calculated to 5.40 nM via the formula of 3σ/k [61], which was far lower than the standard maximum of Hg2+ in drinking water stipulated by the EPA (10 nM) [7]. A comparison of our proposed method with recently reported sensing strategies of Hg2+ was summarized in Table S1, the results manifested that M-CDs was comparative with even superior to some reported fluorescent probes, manifested the feasibility of detecting Hg2+ of M-CDs.

The performance of removal was successively estimated after determined the existence of Hg2+, and the ICP test was employed to validate the effect of separation. The two removal strategies were carried out by dry and wet method to evaluate their removal behavior of Hg2+. Firstly, the wet process carried out, and the Hg2+ contents of samples were analyzed by ICP as demonstrated in Fig. 7a and Table S2. After adding 20.00 ppm Hg2+ solution into ultrapure water with the volume ratio of 1:1, the finding concentration of Hg2+ was 10.04 ppm (sample I, Table S2). The sample II was first mixing the 20.00 ppm Hg2+ solution with M-CDs probe, then removal operation performed by a permanent magnet for 24 h, finally the supernatant was collected for analysis. The ICP testing was measured with concentration of Hg2+ was 7.72 ppm. In comparison, the Hg2+ content was decreased evidently, which provided strong evidence of removing Hg2+ from water samples with an effectiveness of 22.80%. To evaluate the mechanism of magnetic removal of Hg2+, a control experiment was carried out under the same conditions by the pure Fe3O4 NPs (sample III). The separation effectiveness was only 3.70 %, manifested the Fe3O4 NPs could not remove the Hg2+ effectively, the combination with CDs improved the collection function for Hg2+ [46]. A possible process was supposed that Hg2+ firstly combined with GSH-CDs wrapped on the surface to form M-CDs-Hg2+ aggregates, which facilitated the separation with external magnetic field. This assumption was further confirmed by images, the sample attracted by the permanent magnet showed clear solution with evident brown precipitate in daylight (Fig. S5, right), corresponding image under UV light (right) illustrated the weaker blue FL than original sample. To further evaluate the feasibility of removal behavior in real water, the study of magnetic removal was performed at the different temperatures (Table S3) to meet the need of complicated environment. The magnetic removal process can be accomplished at 15 °C, 25 °C and 35 °C with effective capture of Hg2+, and the result indicated higher temperature facilitating the separation behavior (Fig. 7b). The above findings illustrated the excellent potential applicable performance of M-CDs.

Meanwhile, the dry process exhibited more convenient operation. The measured concentration of Hg2+ by ICP was descended from original 10.04–0.17 ppm, the removal of Hg2+ can reach 98.30 % (Fig. 7c), indicated the excellent removal behavior of M-CDs. The reusability of M-CDs was further evaluated by recovering the ability of removal by EDTA saturated solution (Table S4). The reusable removal capacity was verified after 3 cycles, the removal effectiveness 96.60 % and 84.10 % were obtained in the second and third turns (Fig. 7c), manifested the Hg2+ can be removed significantly by dry process. The third recycle manifested their removal behavior descended but still is comparative with even better than many reported materials (Table S5) [38,44,46,56]. The above results confirmed the sustainable utility of M-CDs can be performed at least 3 times with excellent removal effectiveness, which provided a potential application in both sensing and removal of the Hg2+ in environmental water.

To evaluate the removal efficiency of M-CDs in complicated environmental samples, the removal of Hg2+ (10 ppm, 100 ppm) from the coexistence of all other ions (resultant concentration was 357.14 μM) sample was investigated. As delineated in Table S6, the removal efficiency can reach 98.61% (original 10 ppm) and 95.62 % (original 100 ppm) by dry process, and the wet process only 17.43 %, which was echoed the results of Table S5. These results can not only verify the environmental tolerance of M-CDs, but also prove the validity of removal.

For the purpose of estimating the practical feasibility of detection and removal in environmental samples, Hg2+ levels in real water samples from yellow sea and tap water were evaluated by standard addition methods. As shown in Fig. S6, there no Hg2+ was found in the as collected yellow sea and tap water samples. As listed in Table S7, the recovery rates of Hg2+ obtained by FL analysis were 93.90–106.60 % and the relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 3) were lower than 5.64 %, demonstrating the M-CDs can achieve quantitative detection for Hg2+ in real water sample, indicated their positive environmental tolerance and applicable capacity.

To elucidate the dual-functional performance of M-CDs, the detection mechanism for Hg2+ by M-CDs should be discussed by evaluating their absorption and fluorescent decay. Fig. 8a exhibited the excitation spectrum of M-CDs and absorption of Hg2+. Apparently, the Hg2+ showed strong absorption band at around 300 nm, which overlapped with the M-CDs excitation band at λex = 360 nm, this finding could exclude the quenching mechanism of charge transfer firmly [30]. Therefore, the Hg2+ induced FL quenching might be interpreted by the result of FL resonance energy transfer (FRET) or IFE [22,62]. For the better comprehension of the underlying quenching mechanism, the FL lifetime was further measured. The fluorescent decay curves of M-CDs (Fig. 8b) with and without 100 μM of Hg2+ were fitted by a triple exponential function: I(t) = A1 exp(−t/τ1) +A2 exp(−t/τ2) +A3 exp(−t/τ3), where τ1, τ2 and τ3 represent the decay time constants of different radiative decay channels and A1, A2 and A3 are the corresponding amplitudes [17]. Based on fitting data, the average fluorescent lifetime was obtained according to the equation as follows:
(1) τaverage = (A1τ1 2+A2τ2 2+A3τ3 2)/ (A1τ1+A2τ2+A3τ3)

The FL decay profiles of M-CDs and M-CDs+Hg2+ with almost unchanged with FL lifetimes of 9.38 and 9.22 ns, respectively. These results manifested that the quenching mechanism belonged to the IFE mechanism instead of FRET mechanism, because of no significant shortening of FL lifetime emerging [61].

IFE generally referred to that the excitation or emission spectra of the fluorophores were absorbed by the quencher, so as to reduce or even quench the FL intensity [62], which indicated the absorption position of Hg2+ existed great possibility to eliminate the emission of probe easily, caused the quenching of the FL of M-CDs. Owing to FL quenching is generally determined by the coexistence of several parameters [22], the possibility of dynamic quenching effect (DQE) and SQE mechanism was also discussed in this work. The FL quenching was analyzed via the Stern-Volmer equation [46] as follows:
(2) I0/I = 1+Ksv[Q] = 1+Kq∙τ0[Q]
where I and I0 represent the FL intensity of M-CDs with and without Hg2+, respectively; [Q] represents the concentration of Hg2+, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, Kq denotes the quenching rate constant, and τ 0 is the lifetime (9.38 ns). Based on the linear equation (I0/I = 1.59x+1.01, R2 = 0.999), the Stern-Volmer constant Ksv corresponded to 1.59 × 106 L−1∙mol−1. According to Ksv = Kq/τ0, Kq was calculated to be 1.70 × 1014 L−1∙M−1∙s−1, which is higher than the maximum diffusion-controlled limit (2.0 × 1010 M−1·s−1), demonstrating SQE mechanism of the process [46]. According to the reported literature, the formation of a non-fluorescent complex occurs between fluorophore and quenching agent in SQE process [23]. Thus, the FL quenching of M-CDs may be caused by the chelating reaction of Hg2+ with functional groups on the M-CDs surface [57]. As shown in Fig. 8c, the FT-IR of M-CDs without and with Hg2+ were compared. After reaction with Hg2+, the C-N bending modes was strengthened and significant blue-shifted to 1385 cm−1. In addition, the C-S stretch vibration band at 1124 cm−1 and the C-O at 1043 cm−1 was blue-shifted to 1117 cm−1 and 996 cm−1. Notably, the carbonyl group at 1655 cm−1 not only occur evident blue shift, but also weakened the intensity of the stretching vibration absorption band. The above results indicated that Hg2+ chelated with M-CDs by C-O, -SH, C-N, N-H and CO groups. As depicted in Fig. 8d, the UV absorption spectrum of M-CDs at 350 nm blue shifted remarkably after the addition of Hg2+, which also supported the above hypothesis. Additionally, the TEM of M-CDs manifested large scale aggregation took place with addition of Hg2+, representing the formation of the non-fluorescent complex of M-CDs-Hg2+ caused the FL quenching (Fig. S7). Therefore, the FL quenching occurred under the common influence of IFE and SQE.

Furthermore, the binding mechanism of M-CDs towards Hg2+ were further explored by XPS analysis to verify the occurrence of SQE. The N 1s spectrum in M-CDs-Hg2+ (Fig. 8e) demonstrated three peaks at 399.8, 401.8 and 406.8 eV can be situated to amino N, pyrrolic N and C-N-Hg bands [12,34,64]. The C 1s peaks in M-CDs-Hg2+ (Fig. S8) located at 284.8, 286.4 and 288.2 eV corresponded to the C-C/CC, C-N/C-S and CO/CN species, respectively [6,47,61]. Compared with M-CDs, the C-N content reduced from 37.7 % to 14.9 %, attributing to the formation of C-N-Hg bond. The O 1s spectrum of M-CDs-Hg2+ (Fig. 8f) showed the existence of Fe-O bond at 530.1 eV, C-O-Hg bond at 531.5 eV, and CO bond at 532.6 eV [34,37,41]. And five peaks were observed by deconvolution of S 2p of M-CDs-Hg2+ (Fig. 8g) at 161.5, 163.6, 164.7, 168.3, 169.3 eV can be assigned to HgS, S 2p3/2, S 2p1/2, C-SO2-C and C-O-SO2-C respectively, verified the existence of Hg-S bond [18,35,42,6]. The Hg 4f (Fig. 8h) could be fitted into three peaks at 100.3 eV, 101.9 eV and 103.3 eV, which corresponded to HgS (Hg 4f7/2), N-Hg/O-Hg and HgS (Hg 4f5/2), respectively [34]. Notably, the remarkable band-shifts was observed for M-CDs-Hg2+ in XPS high resolution spectra, which confirmed the coordination of M-CDs with Hg2+ by the -NH and -SH groups. The emergence of C-N-Hg, C-O-Hg, HgS signals can be proposed bound to M-CDs by C-N, C-O, C-S of M-CDs to undergo SQE (Fig. 8i). Thus, it can be further deduced that the excellent selectivity of M-CDs for Hg2+ was ascribed to the more strong affinity of the C-N, C-O, C-S of M-CDs toward Hg2+ than other metals.

In summary, a dual-functional magnetic M-CDs probe was fabricated by tuning the appropriate ratio of precursors and rational reaction conditions for fluorescent detection and removal of Hg2+ via a successive of process. High QY, paramagnetism and high selectivity for Hg2+ of the M-CDs were synergistically achieved by tuning the glutathione, diethylenetriamine and ammonium ferric citrate precursors of S, N and Fe. The FL of M-CDs was quenched by Hg2+ on account of the SQE and IFE mechanism, with a detection limit of 5.40 nM. Notably, the M-CDs exhibited high effectiveness (98.30%) and reusability, which can be expected as a promising probe for the detection and separation of Hg2+ simultaneously. Due to advantageous features of excellent sensitivity and selectivity, magnetic property, and low detection limit for Hg2+, the novel M-CDs probe exhibits great potential for the rapid sensitive assay and magnetic removal of Hg2+ in actual water systems, and also provides useful guideline for multifunctional probes for water remediation.

Heavy metals are dangerous, their toxicity, their easy accumulation and poor degradability are acknowledged. Heavy metals can enter a water supply by industrial and consumer waste, or even from acidic rain breaking down soils and releasing into streams, lakes, rivers, and groundwater. Monitoring heavy metals in water are crucial to environmental protection and human health. The M-CDs, as a novel magnetic dual function fluorescent probe, achieving the simultaneous detection and removal of Hg2+ via a same process, creating a precedent for the combination of detection and removal and providing a meaningful idea for the treatment of heavy metal pollution.
